metadata.title: Clear and Direct: Why Vague Prompts Produce Vague Outputs

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Clear and Direct: Why Vague Prompts Produce Vague Outputs

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A vague prompt like 'write a summary' allows dozens of valid interpretations; a specific brief eliminates them. Specificity has four dimensions: length, format, audience, and required components. Each unspecified dimension is a degree of freedom Claude fills with a default that may not be what you needed. Treat Claude like a capable new employee with no context, and write a proper brief instead of 'help with this.'

Each unspecified dimension is a degree of freedom Claude fills with a default that may not be what you needed.
Treat Claude like a capable new employee with no context, and write a proper brief instead of 'help with this.'

beats.narration_text: Here is why Clear and Direct: Why Vague Prompts Produce Vague Outputs belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: A vague prompt like 'write a summary' allows dozens of valid interpretations; a specific brief eliminates them.

Each unspecified dimension is a degree of freedom Claude fills with a default that may not be what you needed.

beats.narration_text: The four dimensions of specificity are what I want to drill into with my own weak prompts. Take my request 'write a summary': (1) rewrite it specifying length, format, audience, and required components the way your three-sentence plain-language example does, (2) show me how each dimension I leave blank becomes a degree of freedom Claude fills with a default, and (3) apply the 'capable new employee with no context' test to catch what I assumed but never stated.

props.segment: Clear and Direct: Why Vague Prompts Produce Vague Outputs
props.command: Vague prompts produce vague outputs. That is not a model limitation — it is a specification failure. Claude is a literal instruction-follower. If you leave room for interpretation, it will interpret. The fix is to be specific.

beats.narration_text: Compare two prompts: 'Write a summary' versus 'Write a 3-sentence summary of the following document in plain language suitable for a non-expert reader. Include the main finding, the method, and the limitation.' Both are requesting a summary. One specifies length, register, audience, and three required components. The second prompt eliminates dozens of valid interpretations. That is the value of specificity.

Specificity has four dimensions. Length: how long? Format: what structure? Audience: who is reading? Required components: what must be in the output? Most vague prompts leave all four unspecified. Each unspecified dimension is a degree of freedom for Claude to fill with its default. Default may not be wrong — but it may not be what you needed. Specify every dimension you care about.

The golden rule: treat Claude like a capable new employee who has no context. A new employee does not know your house style, your audience assumptions, or your edge-case preferences. Tell them explicitly. The difference between a vague prompt and a specific one is the difference between 'help with this' and a proper brief. Write the brief.

Your turn. Take a prompt you use regularly — something you have been running as-is. Audit it against the four dimensions: length, format, audience, required components. Add specifications for any dimension you care about but left unspecified. Run both versions and compare. The specificity you added is the instruction you were giving implicitly by expecting Claude to guess. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
